title: Tunable Dual-Type Weyl Points in Dirac-Weyl Semimetal CaAgBi
authors: Shenghao Huang, Heng Gao, Hongfei Wang, Wei Ren
abstract: Dirac-Weyl semimetals host both Dirac and Weyl fermions and the exploration of material candidates with tunable topological properties is essential to realize topological spintronic devices. In this work, we propose CaAgBi as a Dirac-Weyl semimetal with tunable type-I and type-II Weyl points based on first-principle calculations. In addition to the three pairs of Dirac points located along the rotational axis as previously reported, our calculations reveal 18 additional pairs of dual-type Weyl points distributed across three distinct planes: type-I in the $k_z=0$ plane and type-II in the $k_z= \pm 0.0698\,\frac{2π}{c}$ planes. The topological features are further confirmed through chirality of the Weyl points as well as the existence of surface Fermi arcs. Moreover, we find that the position and annihilation of Dirac and Weyl points are tunable by the alloy engineering and external strains. The alloy engineering is employed to modulate the positions of Weyl points, revealing different annihilation concentrations for type-I and type-II Weyl points, potentially offering novel experimental strategies for Weyl point manipulation. Under tensile biaxial strain, the Weyl points in the $k_{z}=0$ plane annihilate along the $Γ$--$\mathrm{M}$ path at $2.1\%$ strain, whereas the Weyl points in the $k_{z}\neq 0$ planes remain robust within $6\%$ strain. This work provides a versatile platform for manipulating Dirac/Weyl interactions, with spin-orbit coupling (SOC) driven alloy control and strain-selective engineering opening avenues for topological electronics.
summary: This paper identifies a new class of Dirac-Weyl semimetals that naturally host both Type-I and Type-II Weyl fermions. It demonstrates that these topological features can be selectively manipulated using strain and chemical doping, providing a platform for designing topological spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the coexistence, distribution, and tunability of Dirac and Weyl points (both Type-I and Type-II) in the Dirac-Weyl semimetal CaAgBi.
main_result: The researchers discovered 18 pairs of dual-type Weyl points and demonstrated that their positions and annihilation can be controlled via alloy engineering and external biaxial strain.
method: First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP package, combined with tight-binding models via Wannier functions and k.p effective Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: The primary system is the noncentrosymmetric, Wurtzite-like hexagonal crystal CaAgBi (space group P63mc). The study also explores the CaAgBixSb1-x alloy system and the high-pressure phase CaAuBi.
key_observables: Weyl point types (Type-I and Type-II), Berry curvature/chirality, surface Fermi arcs, and band inversion.
important_parameters: Bi concentration (x), biaxial tensile strain (up to 6%), and hydrostatic pressure (up to 10 GPa).
main_assumptions: The alloy properties are modeled using the Virtual Crystal Approximation (VCA) with linear interpolation.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate the distribution of Dirac and Weyl points in the Brillouin zone, the comparison between DFT and tight-binding band structures for CaAuBi, and the density of surface states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material properties, details the computational methods, presents the discovery of new Weyl point pairs, analyzes the effects of alloy engineering and strain on topological stability, and concludes with potential applications in topological electronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The crystal structure of hexagonal CaAgBi. The blue, grey, and purple balls indicate Ca, Ag, and Bi atoms, respectively. (b) Brillouin zone and high symmetry points and paths.
